questions.question: Explain the difference between NAS and SAN storage architectures.
keyPoints: NAS provides file-level storage accessed over TCP/IP network
SAN provides block-level storage using Fibre Channel or iSCSI
NAS is simpler, cheaper but slower; SAN is faster, complex and expensive
NAS appears as shared folders; SAN appears as local disks

questions.question: What is file locking and why is it important in distributed file systems?
keyPoints: File locking prevents simultaneous write access to the same file
Ensures data consistency and prevents corruption
Two types: advisory locking (voluntary) and mandatory locking (enforced by OS)
Important for concurrent access scenarios in client-server environments

questions.question: Describe the role of VFS (Virtual File System) in operating systems.
keyPoints: VFS provides abstraction layer between user applications and actual file systems
Enables multiple different file systems to coexist in the same OS
Provides uniform API for file operations regardless of underlying file system type
Supports local (ext4, NTFS) and network (NFS, CIFS) file systems transparently

questions.question: Explain the mount operation in NFS.
keyPoints: Mount attaches a remote file system to a local directory tree
Client sends mount request to server with export path
Server validates client permissions and exports the directory
After mount, remote files appear as local directory contents

questions.question: Compare stateful and stateless file servers.
keyPoints: Stateful: maintains client session info, open file handles, better performance through caching
Stateless: treats each request independently, easier recovery after failure, simpler design
NFSv2/v3 are stateless; NFSv4 introduced stateful operations
Choice affects reliability, scalability, and complexity

questions.question: What are the advantages of file caching in distributed file systems?
keyPoints: Reduces network traffic by storing frequently accessed data locally
Improves response time by eliminating remote access for cached data
Trade-off: cache coherency issues must be managed
Types: write-through (safe but slow) and write-back (fast but risky)

questions.question: Explain the purpose of file replication in file service architecture.
keyPoints: Creates multiple copies of files on different servers
Improves reliability and fault tolerance
Enhances availability - users can access from nearest replica
Used in enterprise and cloud storage systems for data protection
